revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Chikushi-jinja]][[ja:筑紫神社]] {{Expand Japanese|筑紫神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Chikushi Shrine | native_name = 筑紫神社 | image = Chikushi-jinja keidai.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Chikushi Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|33.457049|130.542851}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Chikushi Shrine'''|筑紫神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Chikuzen Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Myojin Taisha|lt=Myōjin Taisha|en|Myojin Taisha|fr|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|simple|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} * {{ill|Kokuhei-sha|lt=Kokuhei-sha|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Hiteisha|lt=Shikinai Hiteisha|ja|比定社|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11602776|ltq=Q11602776|qid=Q11602776|1=List of Shikinaisha in Chikuzen Province}} ** Series ordinal: 7 ** P155: {{ill|Q135041469|ltq=Q135041469|qid=Q135041469|1=Shitono Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Kamado Shrine|lt=Kamado Shrine|en|Kamado Shrine|fr|Kamado-jinja|ja|竈門神社|simple|Kamado Shrine|qid=Q11597744|1=Kamado Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q844140|ltq=Q844140|qid=Q844140|1=Chikushino, Fukuoka}} * {{ill|Q1047664|ltq=Q1047664|qid=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}} * {{ill|Mikasa district|lt=Mikasa district|ja|御笠郡|pl|Powiat Mikasa|zh|御笠郡|qid=Q11071211|1=Mikasa district}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|33.457049|130.542851}} == image (P18) == * Chikushi-jinja keidai.JPG == described by source (P1343) == * {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database (old)|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database (old)|qid=Q135037878|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database (old)}} ** P2699: https://web.archive.org/web/20060723183943/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp:80/db/jinja/580401.html == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 183296 ** P3831: {{ill|Q135159299|ltq=Q135159299|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q90139226|ltq=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|1=Ken-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == P14005 (P14005) == * {{ill|Q11071125|ltq=Q11071125|qid=Q11071125|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} * {{ill|Q11071127|ltq=Q11071127|qid=Q11071127|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * ちくしじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 6290005006529 == Commons category (P373) == * Chikushi-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福岡県筑紫野市原田2550', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Chikushi-jinja}} [[JA:筑紫神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11602932}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Chikushi Shrine |Image = [[File:Chikushi-jinja keidai.JPG|260px]]
Precincts |Location = {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Chikushino City|Q844140|qid=Q844140|1=Chikushino, Fukuoka}}{{ill|lt=Harada (Chikushino City)|Q11409733|qid=Q11409733|lt=Harada|1=Harada}} 2550 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Deity of Chikushi |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Grand Shrine|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = |Other names = |Pilgrimage sites etc. = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 20|Q2959|qid=Q2959|1=October 20}} |Rituals = Kayūra Festival ({{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|qid=Q2403|1=March 15}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Chikushi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} [[File:Chikushi-jinja ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First torii gate}}]] '''Chikushi Shrine''' (ちくしじんじゃ / つくしじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Chikushino City|Q844140|qid=Q844140|1=Chikushino, Fukuoka}}{{ill|lt=Harada (Chikushino City)|Q11409733|qid=Q11409733|lt=Harada|1=Harada}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three kamiThe list of deities is based on the shrine’s official history.. * '''Chikushi no Kami''' (つくしのかみ) – the {{ill|lt=kunitama|Q9692242|qid=Q9692242|1=Kunitama}} (tutelary spirit) of {{ill|lt=Chikushi Province|Q1206461|qid=Q1206461|lt=Chikushi|1=Tsukushi Province}}. * {{ill|lt=Tamayorihime (Hyūga mythology)|Q3082751|qid=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} – later invited from {{ill|lt=Kamado Shrine|Q11597744|qid=Q11597744|1=Kamado Shrine}}. * {{ill|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979|qid=Q941979|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} – later enshrined together (gōshi). === About the enshrined deities === An excerpt from the *Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki*, quoted in the {{ill|lt=Shaku Nihongi|Q2710483|qid=Q2710483|1=Shaku Nihongi}}, states that {{ill|lt=Chikugo Province|Q864159|qid=Q864159|1=Chikugo Province}} was originally one province together with {{ill|lt=Chikuzen Province|Q1047664|qid=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}}, forming {{ill|lt=Chikushi Province|Q1206461|qid=Q1206461|1=Tsukushi Province}}. As for the origin of the name “Chikushi,” three theories are recorded: (1) the mountain pass between the two provinces was so steep that saddles were worn out, hence “kurazukushi” (“saddles exhausted”); (2) a violent deity dwelled at the border of the two provinces, causing travelers to lose their lives—thus “inochi-zukushi” (“lives exhausted”)—until the deity was pacified through the rites of the Chikushi no Kimi and Higo no Kimi; and (3) to mourn the many deaths in the previous theory, coffins were made and the mountains were stripped of trees, giving rise to “kizukushi” (“trees exhausted”). Among these, a connection between the second theory and Chikushi Shrine’s enshrined deity, Chikushi no Kami, has been pointed out. {{ill|lt=Motoori Norinaga|Q384647|qid=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}} regarded the “inochi-zukushi” theory as the most plausible in his {{ill|lt=Kojikiden|Q11410835|qid=Q11410835|1=Kojiki-den}}. Although it is unclear whether these legends are directly related to the founding of Chikushi Shrine, particular attention is paid to the tradition that the shrine was worshipped by the Chikushi no Kimi ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chikushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|1=UNKNOWN}}) and the Higo no Kimi ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|1=UNKNOWN}}). While this area lay within the sphere of influence of the Chikushi no Kimi, the advance of the Higo no Kimi from central Kyushu to northern Kyushu was triggered by the {{ill|lt=Iwai Rebellion|Q3138004|qid=Q3138004|1=Iwai Rebellion}} in the mid-6th century, and this tradition is considered to reflect ritual relations after that advance. Furthermore, from the theory that Shirahiwake no Kami and {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}} were the ancestral deities of the Chikushi and Higo provincial rulers, it is assumed that the {{ill|lt=Chikushi clan|Q11602927|qid=Q11602927|1=Chikushi clan}} of the {{ill|lt=Shōni clan|Q2975368|qid=Q2975368|1=Shōni clan}} lineage, which later served Chikushi Shrine, succeeded the position of the Chikushi no Kimi, descendants of the provincial governors{{ill|lt=Hōga Toshio|Q11452691|qid=Q11452691|1=Toshio Hōga}} “6. Early sections of the Okinaga-related clans,” *Studies on Ancient Clans ⑥: The Okinaga Clan—A Smithing Clan that Produced Great Kings*, Aogaki Publishing, 2014, pp. 123–124.. In addition, there are theories identifying Chikushi no Kami as Shirahiwake no Kami or as {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10881556|lt=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}. * Shirahiwake no Kami theory *: Proposed by Yoshimura Chiharu. In the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}} {{ill|lt=Kuniumi|Q3545163|qid=Q3545163|lt=creation myth|1=Kuniumi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tsukushi Island|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kyushu) is described as having four faces: {{ill|lt=Chikushi Province|Q1206461|qid=Q1206461|1=Tsukushi Province}}, {{ill|lt=Toyokuni|Q1205043|qid=Q1205043|1=Toyo Province}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Higo Province|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|qid=Q1196897|lt=Kumaso Province|1=Kumaso}}, and it is stated that “Chikushi Province is called Shirahiwake.” Since the Chinese character “白” (white) was used as a phonetic loan in Korea to represent light, brightness, or the sun, a connection with Korea has been suggested*Gods of Japan*, entry on Chikushi Shrine.. * Isotakeru-no-Mikoto theory *: Proposed by Matsushita Kenrin and {{ill|lt=Kaibara Ekiken|Q11452691|qid=Q11452691|lt=Kaibara Ekiken|1=Toshio Hōga}}. In an alternative divine-age tradition in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} descends to Silla accompanied by Isotakeru-no-Kami (Isotakeru-no-Mikoto), later moving to Izumo. Isotakeru-no-Kami is said to have possessed many kinds of trees, but did not plant them in Korea, instead sowing them throughout the country beginning in Chikushi. From these legends, Isotakeru-no-Kami is thought to have been a deity of immigrant origin, again suggesting a connection with Korea. === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. There are theories that the shrine was originally worshipped on the summit of Mount Shiroyama and later moved to the foothills, or that it was enshrined at the current location from the beginningRelocation theory: *Zoku Fudoki Shūi*; non-relocation theory: *Zoku Fudoki* (both cited in *Place Names of Fukuoka Prefecture*, entry on Chikushi Shrine).. The name “Chikushi” in a broad sense referred to the whole of Kyushu, as in “Chikushi Island” ({{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}) or “Chikushi Province” ({{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}), but in a narrower sense it is said to refer to the place name around Chikushi Shrine. Archaeologically, the low hilly area on the eastern side of the Sefuri Mountains around this site was a central area for jar-coffin burials in the Middle Yayoi period, and for bronze production in the Late Yayoi period. The origins of dōtaku (bronze bell) rituals are also thought to lie in this region, and their flourishing would have required immigrant knowledge; taken together with the traditions of Shirahiwake and Isotakeru-no-Mikoto, ritual practices by immigrant groups have been suggested. === Outline history === The earliest mention in the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|qid=Q1151168|lt=official histories|1=Rikkokushi}} records that in the first year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}}) the shrine received the divine rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and in the third year of {{ill|lt=Gangyō|Q747451|qid=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} ({{ill|lt=879|Q25001|qid=Q25001|1=879}}) it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* (compiled in {{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), within the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}*, it is listed under {{ill|lt=Mikasa District|Q11071211|qid=Q11071211|1=Mikasa district}} of {{ill|lt=Chikuzen Province|Q1047664|qid=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}} as “Chikushi Shrine, Myōjin Taisha,” placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}. A government order from the second year of {{ill|lt=Tengen (Japan)|Q1327101|qid=Q1327101|lt=Tengen|1=Tengen (era)}} ({{ill|lt=979|Q23784|qid=Q23784|1=979}}) records that a chief priest (daigūji) post was established at Chikushi Shrine along with {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka City)|Q29682|qid=Q29682|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)}}, {{ill|lt=Kashii Shrine|Q631373|qid=Q631373|1=Kashii-gū}}, {{ill|lt=Kamado Shrine|Q11597744|qid=Q11597744|1=Kamado Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Hakozaki Shrine|Q714742|qid=Q714742|1=Hakozaki Shrine}}. From the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|qid=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the {{ill|lt=Chikushi clan|Q11602927|qid=Q11602927|1=Chikushi clan}}, who served as local stewards (jitō), also served as shrine priests. Later, during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|qid=Q204023|lt=Warring States period|1=Sengoku period}}, the shrine buildings and ancient documents were burned in warfare by the forces of the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|qid=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, and the shrine fell into ruin due to bandit attacks. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, restoration efforts were undertaken and the shrine buildings were rebuilt. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in the fifth year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|qid=Q7753|1=1872}}) the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, but became a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} following administrative reforms. In Meiji 14 ({{ill|lt=1881|Q7808|qid=Q7808|1=1881}}) it was again promoted to Gōsha, and in the fourth year of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} ({{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|qid=Q2084|1=1915}}) it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}Shrine official history.. === Divine ranks === * {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}}), January 27: Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}*) * {{ill|lt=Gangyō|Q747451|qid=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=879|Q25001|qid=Q25001|1=879}}), June 8: Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) === Shrine precincts === File:Chikushi-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Chikushi-jinja haiden.JPG|Worship hall File:Chikushi-jinja sandou.JPG|Shrine gate and approach File:Chikushi-jinja torii.JPG|Stone torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Chikushi-jinja Gosho-jinja.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Gosho Shrine}}]] * Gosho Shrine (ごしょじんじゃ) ** Enshrined deities: Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Kushinada-hime, Sugawara no Michizane, Izanagi no Ōkami, Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto *: In Taishō 4, five nearby unranked shrines were merged, and in Shōwa 4 the precinct shrines were also merged. {{-}} === Festivals === === Annual festivals === * New Year Festival (January 1–3)Festival information is based on the shrine’s official history. * {{ill|lt=Kayūra divination|Q11604655|qid=Q11604655|lt=Kayūra Festival|1=Kayu ura}} (also called Okayu-ura Festival; Spring Grand Festival) (March 15) * Oyodo (summer festival), Summer purification rite (July 19) * Annual Festival (Autumn Grand Festival) (October 20) === Kayūra Festival === The '''Kayūra Festival''' (かゆうらさい) is held every year on March 15, commonly known as “'''Kayūra'''.” It is a divination ritual that predicts the year’s harvest and outbreaks of disease by observing the {{ill|lt=mold|Q159341|qid=Q159341|1=Mold}} that grows on {{ill|lt=rice porridge|Q878624|qid=Q878624|lt=okayu|1=Congee}}. On February 15, water drawn from the shrine’s {{ill|lt=well|Q43483|qid=Q43483|1=Well}} is used by the shrine priest to cook rice porridge over a {{ill|lt=hearth|Q44539|qid=Q44539|lt=kamado|1=Temple}}. The porridge is then placed in a large {{ill|lt=bowl|Q56351193|qid=Q56351193|1=Hachi}}, and {{ill|lt=chopsticks|Q81980|qid=Q81980|1=Chopsticks}} are laid in a cross on top. The center of the cross represents Chikushi Shrine, and name tags representing areas to the east, west, south, and north as seen from the shrine are inserted into the porridge. The porridge is then placed in the shrine’s {{ill|lt=sanctuary|Q44539|qid=Q44539|1=Temple}} for one month. On March 15, the day of the Kayūra Festival, the priest removes the porridge and judges the condition of the mold on its surface to determine the year’s {{ill|lt=grain|Q12117|qid=Q12117|1=Cereal}} harvest and whether {{ill|lt=infectious diseases|Q2995419|qid=Q2995419|1=Contagious disease}} will spread. The judgments are made according to an ancient Kayūra divination manual preserved by the shrine, and the results are posted at the shrine. This Kayūra Festival has been designated an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Chikushino City[http://www.city.chikushino.fukuoka.jp/kyouikubu/bunka-joho/tikusizinnzyakayuura.html Chikushi Shrine Kayūra Divination Ritual and Porridge Bowl] (Chikushino City website).. === Cultural properties === === Chikushino City–designated Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Chikushi Shrine Kayūra Divination Ritual and Porridge Bowl – Designated November 1, Shōwa 59 (1984)[http://www.city.chikushino.fukuoka.jp/kyouikubu/bunka-joho/shitei-bunkazai_tennenkinenbutsu.html Designated Cultural Properties and Natural Monuments] (Chikushino City website).. === On-site information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Chikushino City|Q844140|qid=Q844140|1=Chikushino, Fukuoka}} Harada 2550 '''Access''' * Railway ** {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|qid=Q498366|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} (JR Kyushu) {{ill|lt=Kagoshima Main Line|Q1187520|qid=Q1187520|1=Kagoshima Main Line}} / {{ill|lt=Chikuhō Main Line|Q872540|qid=Q872540|1=Chikuhō Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Harada Station (Fukuoka Prefecture)|Q5359769|qid=Q5359769|lt=Harada Station|1=Haruda Station}} (about 10 minutes on foot) – 700 m to the northeast ** {{ill|lt=Nishi-Nippon Railroad|Q869251|qid=Q869251|1=Nishi-Nippon Railroad}} (Nishitetsu) {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tenjin Ōmuta Line|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Chikushi Station|Q5097520|qid=Q5097520|1=Chikushi Station}} (20 minutes on foot) – 1,600 m to the southwest === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine official history * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|qid=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}}: Place Names of Fukuoka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|qid=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}), entry on Chikushi Shrine, Chikushino City * Okuno Masao, “Chikushi Shrine” (in {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken’ichi|Q2392596|qid=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), *Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – 1 Kyushu*, {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|qid=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}) === Related literature === * {{ill|lt=Shirai Eiji|Q11579387|qid=Q11579387|1=Eiji Shirai}} and Toki Masanori (eds.), *Dictionary of Shrines*, {{ill|lt=Tōkyōdō Publishing|Q11524645|qid=Q11524645|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan}}, 1979, p. 225 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Chikushi Province|Q1206461|qid=Q1206461|1=Tsukushi Province}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Chikushi-jinja}} * [http://www.kyuhaku-db.jp/dazaifu/historic/48.html Chikushi Shrine] – Kyushu National Museum “Saito Dazaifu” * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/580401.html Chikushi Shrine] – Kokugakuin
